speaker: interviewer
text: OK, just, just we'll film the [? slate ?] part. Not the still. They have to put on-- Is this too-- It's got wheels. [INAUDIBLE] Oh, I mean simple. It's, it's too-- Bench. OK. Well, actually what is-- we need simple one. What about a kitchen chair? There's one under there-- a cushion. No, but he said he want a lower one.

speaker: interviewer
text: B-R-A-U-N. I am conducting an interview with David Landau. The date is November the 1st, 1995. The suburb is Toorak, the-- Melbourne. The state is Victoria, Australia. Can I ask you to state your name and spell your surname, please?

speaker: interviewee
text: David John Landau, L-A-N-D-A-U.

speaker: interviewer
text: Do you have any other names?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes, I did have during the resistance-- Jan [INAUDIBLE]

speaker: interviewer
text: Could you spell [INAUDIBLE] please? Oh, OK. Fine. David, your date of birth, please?

speaker: interviewee
text: 1920, the 1st-- the 15th of March.

speaker: interviewer
text: How old are you?

speaker: interviewer
text: The city of birth and the country, please?

speaker: interviewee
text: Warsaw, Poland.

speaker: interviewer
text: Thank you. I have in my hand some writing, a sheet of paper, with--

speaker: interviewer
text: Excuse me. Brenda, you're using your hands.

speaker: interviewer
text: Sorry.

speaker: interviewer
text: Yeah.

speaker: interviewer
text: OK.

speaker: interviewer
text: [INAUDIBLE] .

speaker: interviewer
text: I have in my hand a writing-- a sheet of paper, that David has asked me to read out, his philosophies of thought in reflection to his history. 'In the late evening hours, I sometimes sit down to contemplate over the past. In such moments, I sometime plan also for the future. Lately, I begin these contemplations by admitting to myself that there is such-- there is much more accumulated in my past than I will ever accumulate in my future.

speaker: interviewee
text: The memories have begun to outweigh the plans, and there is nothing abnormal in that. What is out of the norm is the cage of the past in which I am locked in for the rest of my life. The cage does not exist outside my mind, but it is as real to me as if it were made of steel rods, cast in concrete, and fenced off with barbed wire. If, by some chance in the natural laws, I were to live for 1,000 years, I would still remain caged in that invisible jail for the full 1,000 years. I am not the only one trapped in such a cage. I belong to a breed of people which I have never-- which have never existed before my generation and will be extinct from the human race within the next 20 to 30 years. All of our breed suffer from the same apparition. The breed is called survivors, because we cheated the huge Nazi killing machine of death camps and ghettos and came out alive from places where we were meant not to survive. We live in a time warp from which only death will release us, and each of us carries within the mind an imagery cage which dominates our lives. Those who have become my friends or who have known me after I passed the age of 25 consider fate that always smiles on me. This is not the whole truth. They don't know my younger years when fate and I were locked together for years in one cage. And we lived in an endless fight, a knife fight-- fate against me and I against fate. Not I, but fate gave in. Instead of making me its victim, it became friendly to me. And I became a survivor. Only then, it became my friend. But the imagery-- imaginary, beg your pardon-- cage has remained. The steel bars have been covered over the normal life in a free country. But it is still a cage. I welcome you as a visitor to relive with me the years in which fate and I fought the battles of life and death with unsheathed knives. You will not be able to learn it from the first-hand after my generation will die out. The formative years-- three large cities found their special place in the history of the Second World War. They suffered the cruelest form of torture at a time when torture was the norm. Starvation was endemic and cruelty. Deprivation and death visited tens of millions of people. Stalingrad was the first of them. Its name became a synonym of bravery and death, of the fateful, final decision of the world. At Stalingrad, the human will to live overcame the power of total evil on its march to enslave the world for 1,000 years with its plan to wipe out whole races of human species, beginning with the Jews. Berline was the last of the three. From that city, the evil beast began its murderous conquering march. And in Berlin, it was finally put to death.' That's it.

speaker: interviewer
text: David, that's a powerful introduction. This is your thoughts. But I'd like to ask you to begin your life as a child with me. Can you tell us something of how your childhood was? Please introduce your parents and your family.

speaker: interviewee
text: [PAUSES FOR 9 SECONDS] Mother, Miriam. Father, Yitzak. Brother, Eliazur. Brother, Metek. Brother, Juziek. And sister, Sylvia.

speaker: interviewer
text: What type of a life did all of you lead together at home?

speaker: interviewee
text: We're middle class. We'd been-- [PAUSES FOR 6 SECONDS]

speaker: interviewer
text: David, that's fine. I'll just [INAUDIBLE].

speaker: interviewee
text: We'd been engaged in tanneries and in building trade. It was a home not religious. We been Jews, having friends, Jews and Christians but knowing that we're Jewish quite well. Mother's side was all-- including my grandfather-- socialist, more to the left. Father was not involved with socialism, but in his business and his home and children. I lived in a very comfortable, very comfortable life. We did have a very comfortable life-- parents which never screamed at us, never put their hand on us. Our punishment was done in a different way, where if we did want something and we did something which wasn't right, that was refused to give us something or to help us in the way. That was the punishment. Nothing else-- no screaming, [? nothing. ?]

speaker: interviewer
text: Can you tell us from your brothers and sisters, how you interacted with them, played with them, spoke to them? We been all three years apart. When I was, for example, 12, my older brother was 15, and the oldest, 18. The younger brother was nine and sister, six. That was always, that every three years, a child-- five children. Did you-- you went to school?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes, I went.

speaker: interviewer
text: And what type of a school did you go to?

speaker: interviewee
text: I went to school, to private school and then went to a gymnasium. I haven't been very good at school. And at the age of about 15, I left school and have a private tutor in my home. I always been looking to be together with father in the business. I never did want to go to school and be happy at school. So we decided that the best way would be that I'll have a tutor home and help father in the business, which I did.

speaker: interviewer
text: What type of-- did your-- were you from a religious background?

speaker: interviewee
text: Pardon?

speaker: interviewer
text: Was your family traditional or non-religious?

speaker: interviewee
text: Mother's completely not religious, mother's family, including my grandfather. No.

speaker: interviewer
text: So did you celebrate the [? Yom Tov ?] then?

speaker: interviewee
text: No.

speaker: interviewer
text: Fine. With your brothers and sisters, did you go on an outing that had a particular significance to you?

speaker: interviewee
text: Pardon?

speaker: interviewer
text: Did you go out with your brothers and sisters that had a special meaning to you?

speaker: interviewee
text: Not specially. We used to go out to some events, sporting events, but no. We, we kept-- everyone kept for himself. Because three years difference, and the age was quite a bit at the age of 12, 15, 18. So we kept to ourselves.

speaker: interviewer
text: David, in the 1930s, did the life in Warsaw change from what it was a little earlier? Are you aware of this?

speaker: interviewee
text: From 1933, I would say, we've seen a little bit changes with our good friend Adolf being on another side in Germany. Yes, we did found some little incidents here and there.

speaker: interviewer
text: Could you explain one or two of these incidents, please?

speaker: interviewee
text: At universities, I heard from mine friends and brothers that something is happening. And--

speaker: interviewer
text: Such as?

speaker: interviewee
text: Such as excesses like beating up of the Jewish students and things like that. But that was in a small form at that very time.

speaker: interviewer
text: That beating up of the Jewish students, was that conducted by other students at the university?

speaker: interviewee
text: By other students, yes-- by the national organization, the students belonging to the national organization, which did have a little, a little medal in their--

speaker: interviewer
text: Lapel.

speaker: interviewee
text: Lapel which was called [POLISH SPEECH].

speaker: interviewer
text: Could you translate, please?

speaker: interviewee
text: Pardon?

speaker: interviewer
text: Could you translate?

speaker: interviewee
text: That's a sword of one of the kings.

speaker: interviewer
text: And could you describe another incident in the change in the '30s?

speaker: interviewee
text: That wasn't a very happy-- in football matches-- or soccer which is called here-- soccer matches, when it was playing Jewish club with a Christian club, it was calls from the public which wasn't very sympathetic for Jews. We seen a little, little change in it.

speaker: interviewer
text: If you went on an outing with your parents, what would that outing be? If you-- if the family went out, or if you went out with your parents, were would you go?

speaker: interviewee
text: You know, I been, I been always going. If I went up sometimes, I went myself to the [? cabana ?] for skiing. Because I was a skier from the age of six.

speaker: interviewer
text: Yes.

speaker: interviewee
text: I said that, you know, from the age of six, I been a skier. And sometimes, father and mother came with me to the [? cabana, ?] which was about 400 kilometers from Warsaw.

speaker: interviewer
text: How many kilometers?

speaker: interviewee
text: 400 Kilometers approximately.

speaker: interviewee
text: From Warsaw. I think that was about 400 kilometers.

speaker: interviewer
text: And up to what-- up to when were you still able to ski?

speaker: interviewee
text: To ski? I been skiing till 1938.

speaker: interviewer
text: And when you went in 1938, was there a change of conditions there?

speaker: interviewee
text: Everything-- the condition changed from year to year, yes, in mine, mine observation, yes.

speaker: interviewer
text: With the--

speaker: interviewee
text: Because I did have a lot of Christian friends. And I noticed it was a change, yes.

speaker: interviewer
text: And could you describe how you felt the change, please?

speaker: interviewee
text: Being inclined to socialism, I could see that I prefer practically to split it, like, to be separate from the Jewish socialists. And in some cases, I discovered that very left socialism can be quite anti-Semitic. This mean that I did have to wait till 1953 where Khruschev said plainly that as many boys will be at university, that many boys I like to see-- Jewish boys I like to see in the mines. So I did have to wait till 1953 to be convinced what I thought in 1938, '39, what I've been thinking of it, that that was right. Mine, mine, assumption, assumption-- whatever you call it-- was right at that time. That can exist. We did see slowly different attitude towards the Jews.

speaker: interviewer
text: David, you--

speaker: interviewee
text: Because the propaganda of Adolf came deep and deeper to Poland.

speaker: interviewer
text: David, you told me your parents had different political views. So how did they have shalom in the home, peace in the home?

speaker: interviewee
text: Oh, my mother was a very peaceful being, left-- She was a very peaceful woman. And I don't know. I haven't heard anything between them. She only accept what I did, that I was to side with mother. And she was very happy with it. And at one stage, as a boy of 16 and a half, I went-- I tried to go to Spain. I went as far as [? Lyon. ?] And I was turned back because that was signed-- the night was signed the Pact of Nonaggression.

speaker: interviewer
text: Nonaggression.

speaker: interviewee
text: And every boy which was under the military age or not in the army was sent back to Czechoslovakia. The ones who had been over had been smuggled back to Spain.

speaker: interviewer
text: OK. You said you went as far as Lyon. What country?

speaker: interviewee
text: That was the border of France and Spain.

speaker: interviewer
text: France and Spain. Thank you.

speaker: interviewee
text: Basque country.

speaker: interviewer
text: Thank you. Oh, yea-- Basque country. David, give me-- could you please describe whether you were in any youth organizations or interacted in a sporting field? And if so, what sporting field?

speaker: interviewee
text: What sporting--

speaker: interviewer
text: Did you play sport, or were you with any youth movements that you belonged to?

speaker: interviewee
text: No. I been the representative of Warsaw ski team in 1938. And that was the only thing I knew.

speaker: interviewer
text: And were you a member of any youth movements?

speaker: interviewee
text: Of?

speaker: interviewer
text: Any youth organizations?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes. I been around, yes, yes. [INAUDIBLE].

speaker: interviewer
text: What is--

speaker: interviewee
text: This is the socialist organization from the young, young ones-- the, the, the young generation, like 15, 16-year-olds.

speaker: interviewer
text: Was that Jewish-- only Jewish, or mixed?

speaker: interviewee
text: No. Mixed. That was the socialist organization.

speaker: interviewer
text: Right. Right. David, Poland was-- the Germans marched into Poland very early.

speaker: interviewee
text: The Germans?

speaker: interviewer
text: The Germans marched into Poland very early.

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah.

speaker: interviewer
text: So when were you aware of the Polish-- the invasion of Poland? And when did it personally start to affect your life?

speaker: interviewee
text: That's the first day, the first day. Because our tannery start to slow down. Everything start to--

speaker: interviewer
text: When you say the first--

speaker: interviewee
text: One time, [INAUDIBLE] the first thing was to the radio. We'd been told that. And having in the tannery-- already, I been at that time at the tannery-- We knew immediately. And then we did have a phone call from our mother, which was very distressed. And we knew that the war start.

speaker: interviewer
text: When you say the first day, will you please give the year?

speaker: interviewee
text: 1St of September.

speaker: interviewer
text: And the year, please?

speaker: interviewee
text: Pardon?

speaker: interviewer
text: And the year?

speaker: interviewee
text: The year was 1939.

speaker: interviewer
text: Right. So you said the tannery was immediately affected. How?

speaker: interviewee
text: The tannery was affected because we lost few workers which went to the army and immediately called up forthwith. And we did have immediately the secret police there, because we did-- in our place, not knowing-- we did, we did--

speaker: interviewer
text: Harbor.

speaker: interviewee
text: Employ, employ one of the top people, which when the Germans came into Warsaw, we discovered. Ernest or something, [? Franken. ?] He was then a [INAUDIBLE] for, for fur and skins in Poland.

speaker: interviewer
text: He was a what?

speaker: interviewee
text: He was a high ranking SS officer. And he was working with us for about 15 years in the tannery. Nobody knew about it, that he was--

speaker: interviewer
text: Associated with the--

speaker: interviewee
text: A German spy. Yeah. And then immediately when the Germans came in to Warsaw about two, three weeks later, he came to our place asking my father to start off the tannery again, which stopped. Because we did have a sign on the tannery, whoever will come into the tannery is death penalty. The tannery was closed.

speaker: interviewer
text: A death penalty?

speaker: interviewee
text: Death penalty for coming into the tannery, as soon as the German came. Because we been working for the military.

speaker: interviewer
text: The Polish military. OK.

speaker: interviewee
text: The Polish military. So they closed the factory and put a sign. Whoever will pass through the door or open it up is death penalty. That was the first. They didn't only on our factory. They did on every one which was a factory working for the army or for--

speaker: interviewer
text: Could you repeat this person's name, please, David?

speaker: interviewee
text: Pardon?

speaker: interviewer
text: Could you repeat this person's name, if you can remember?

speaker: interviewee
text: If I'm not mistaken, [? Franken ?] for sure-- but Ernest or, or something like that.

speaker: interviewer
text: Thank you. So in the three weeks later when he asked you to begin production, was that production designated for anything particular?

speaker: interviewee
text: Oh, that was for-- would be for, would be for the army. But we didn't open it. My father found any excuse not to open. He brought to us a full cart, a, a, a wagon or whatever you like to call it, with food. Was enough to feed the whole our flats, all the people, for about four weeks. We opened a kitchen from the food which we'd been given from him.

speaker: interviewer
text: In your apartment house?

speaker: interviewee
text: No, in the apartment house, but downstairs.

speaker: interviewer
text: Yes.

speaker: interviewee
text: In the, in the, in the yard. We opened a kitchen. And we fed all the place, because it was very hard to get food at that time.

speaker: interviewer
text: Was-- now, once they marched in and he created this bribe for you, were-- he knew you were Jewish?

speaker: interviewee
text: Oh, yes. He knew that we are Jewish.

speaker: interviewer
text: And he was still-- he still interacted with you?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah, because he did that in the beginning. They wouldn't mind to open any factory which could work for the Germans, factories which have been working for the government, for the army, for things like that. Yes, they would open gladly any one at that time.

speaker: interviewer
text: So he know-- he knew us. And he was in a very high position. So he came in his uniform, beautifully dressed. And he did want to convince my father to start up. But my father found every excuse not to open it. And how did he handle the excuses?

speaker: interviewee
text: Pardon?

speaker: interviewer
text: Did he he try to take-- how did he handle the excuses of your father's refusal to open the factory?

speaker: interviewee
text: He wasn't happy with it. But he took it nice. Because after all, he was working with us for 15 or so years. So he was a little bit, at that time, nicely towards my father. And he said to him that he's not happy and that he prepared to have it start off. But if father doesn't want to, it's bad, bad luck [INAUDIBLE].

speaker: interviewer
text: You mentioned there were shortages of food. Was that all through Warsaw?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes. Right aw-- that was a shortage immediately. When the Germans walked into Warsaw, start immediately queues for bread. And yes, start immediately.

speaker: interviewer
text: Had there been a-- had there been an opportunity to stockpile food of families, or were you unaware of this?

speaker: interviewee
text: Pardon?

speaker: interviewer
text: Was there an opportunity to stockpile food?

speaker: interviewee
text: People did stock. That's why they made the shops empty.

speaker: interviewer
text: Yeah.

speaker: interviewee
text: And the people who did have shops start to hide their little bit of food and sell it slowly out for better prices. Yes, that's, that's nothing unusual in, in, in time of war, that something like that happen. Because after mine experience-- 75 years, I seen it-- that had happen in every country. If there's a war or starting a war, the food is the first thing to be hit. Because that's the only thing which you can get some more money during wars than you get before the war [INAUDIBLE].

speaker: interviewer
text: David, so this was the beginning of the war. David, could you please describe the direction your life went? The factory was stopped. You were three weeks down the track. You were living at home in-- with the family. What happened then?

speaker: interviewee
text: I haven't been with the family all the three weeks. Because after few days, I been-- my brothers had been taken to the army, the older brothers, both of them. One was in the officer school before the war. And one was just a university student which was taken immediately to the army.

speaker: interviewer
text: Could you please expl--

speaker: interviewee
text: Both of them.

speaker: interviewer
text: Could you explain which army this was, please?

speaker: interviewee
text: The Polish army.

speaker: interviewer
text: Thank you.

speaker: interviewee
text: And before the war-- in the start, in the start of the war.

speaker: interviewer
text: Thank you.

speaker: interviewee
text: They have been taken to the army, both of them. And mother was with me, with mine sister and younger brother that was the what left in, at home-- father, mother, and three children. Now, I been told by father that he spoke to a big ranking officer which was in charge with the, the buyer, the military buyer of the-- of our goods for the army. And he told him that he better see me out of Warsaw, the oldest, which is left with-- in the family, David, which was 19-year-old. Better if he will leave Warsaw.

speaker: interviewer
text: If David would leave Warsaw.

speaker: interviewee
text: If I will, David.

speaker: interviewer
text: Yes.

speaker: interviewee
text: And go towards, towards Lublin. Because we're going to have a stand there against the Germans. We're going to re-army, re-organize, And we're going to have a stand near Lublin. So better if a young person will walk to-- will go towards Lublin. And that was a lot of young people you could see at the-- on the road, very, very-- quite a lot when I went out about five, six days after, after the, the war start when the Germans. That was already-- the Germans have been, I think, already in Warsaw, or next to Warsaw. And they start to march out from the-- from Warsaw towards Lublin.

speaker: interviewer
text: So you say you marched out? Were you with--

speaker: interviewee
text: Marched out-- went out with groups of people which were going out of Warsaw. They'd been leaving Warsaw.

speaker: interviewer
text: OK. Were you under the umbrella or under any name?

speaker: interviewee
text: No, no, no.

speaker: interviewer
text: Independent people.

speaker: interviewee
text: Not at all. But on the way, we tried to get into, to places like over the road. We went actually towards Kowel. And on the road, I met few people which I knew, Christian, Jewish people. And then I met up with one boy which was from a village. And we been marching together, together. And few little things happened on, on the way because--

speaker: interviewer
text: Did you take--

speaker: interviewee
text: Not as a Jew, but normally the attacking of the, of the planes, the roads, the German planes. Then we caught two spies.

speaker: interviewer
text: You caught what?

speaker: interviewee
text: Two spies.

speaker: interviewer
text: Oh, two spies.

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah. Dressed in, in clerical, clerical [INAUDIBLE].

speaker: interviewer
text: Clothes.

speaker: interviewee
text: And they been walking in the back of the, of the column and giving signals in the nighttime. Because we been mostly walking in the night. In the day, we used to hide in some of the forest or something which you could hide the whole groups. Hundreds of people who marched-- and army, too, lot of the army, all in trucks or in train or just walking. So they caught them and they-- they caught them seeing that they're giving signals in the nighttime. Because always in the morning, we're being attacked by planes.

speaker: interviewer
text: You were strafed by planes.

speaker: interviewee
text: Immediately in the morning-- 5:00, 6:00 in the morning. Was a little bit light, immediately the planes came in very low over us and attacking with everything, even with bundles of grenades.

speaker: interviewer
text: Even with bundles of grenades?

speaker: interviewee
text: With bundles of grenades from the top, from the airplane.

speaker: interviewer
text: So when you got to-- how long did it take you to get to Lublin? And what happened when you got there?

speaker: interviewee
text: Oh, that took me, I don't know. It's hard to remember now-- quite a few days. We been marching about 30 to 40 kilometers a day.

speaker: interviewer
text: And once you got there, what happened?

speaker: interviewee
text: Once we got there, I got to Kowel. I been there. Then I went to Vilna and then back from Vilna to Kowel and back to Bialystock and home. Because I been told that my mother is very, very ill by some people which came later, friends of mine. And I'd been told [? that dying. ?] I went to a place which was called [INAUDIBLE] and Bartosze in Poland, not far from the border, which was then the border of the Russians with the Germans. I found there some people which I been told about in Warsaw if I will pass through there to see them. And so the people start to think of a little organization, of a little partisan organization. But unfortunately, didn't work long. Didn't work long because we didn't-- we haven't been helped by an organization. We didn't ask any to be helped. But we did want to form ourselves.

speaker: interviewer
text: So--

speaker: interviewee
text: And instead of guns, we did have pieces of sticks which we knocked at the windows and telling the peasants that we are the a group of million people. And we want some food. This was the first thing [INAUDIBLE]. We been given the first two, three days. And then they chased us out of the village. They said if we come again-- most probably, they seen that this is a stick, not a gun. We decide to stop it and to go back home, not to play partisan without an organization.

speaker: interviewer
text: So David, what-- do you remember what village this was? And did you have a name for your little par-- for the partisan group?

speaker: interviewee
text: For the? For the?

speaker: interviewer
text: Did you have a name for the, for the group of you that you called yourself partisans?

speaker: interviewee
text: No. That was about-- no, I can't remember. I been friendly right practically to the war with those two places. And I did have there friends. No, I can't remember names. No.

speaker: interviewer
text: OK. So after that advent, what, what occurred next in your life?

speaker: interviewee
text: And after the war, I tried to go there.

speaker: interviewer
text: Yes.

speaker: interviewee
text: And I been there to both places when I been and start to look for something very wrong. Because I used to say to the people when I've been looking for it-- first of all, my pronunciation wasn't exactly as it should be. Because when you say, for example, Bartosze, a peasant will say Bar-to-sher. It is changing the-- because that's a peasant.

speaker: interviewer
text: The enunciation.

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah. Normally, that's Bartosze and [INAUDIBLE]. Now, I went there. And I made one big mistake when I went after the war to have a look at it.

speaker: interviewer
text: Yes.

speaker: interviewee
text: During the war, everything was all right. I knew where I'm going. But after the war, 19-- that was much later. Because I went out of Poland in 1946, I think-- '45, end '45. And when I went there, it was about 1958, 1960 the first time-- 1958. And I been told by the-- I told the people that's, that's a very important thing. I said, he did have a child of about a year, a boy. And I made a mistake because the child could be already 12-year-old or 15-year-old.

speaker: interviewer
text: Mhm. Yeah.

speaker: interviewee
text: Or whatever it is in 1958. But I made a mistake. And I couldn't find the people.

speaker: interviewer
text: OK. So righty-ho, David-- so you went back. And why did you feel the need to meet this person again?

speaker: interviewee
text: Pardon?

speaker: interviewer
text: Why did you feel the need to meet this person again?

speaker: interviewee
text: When? When I--

speaker: interviewer
text: When you went back after the war?

speaker: interviewee
text: After the war? I told myself knowing the people there and being with them and trying to do something, that I been in Warsaw in my place during the war. So I told myself, I'll have a look. And I'll see what is happening to them.

speaker: interviewer
text: OK.
